description: Running our quarterly integrity audit on /home/user/audit — we have a manifest.sha256 that was generated when the archive was created, and I need to verify every file still matches. Problem is the checker script at check_integrity.py keeps reporting 3 failures but when I manually sha256sum those same files they look fine? The ops team swears nothing was modified. Either the manifest is wrong, the checker is wrong, or something got corrupted — need you to figure out which and get me a clean audit (all files verified, checker exits 0). If files are actually corrupted I need to know that too, but I really don't think they are given the manual checks pass.
truth:
Initial state:
- /home/user/audit/ contains:
  - manifest.sha256: a text file with lines of format `<sha256hex>  <relative_path>` (two spaces between hash and path, standard sha256sum -c format)
  - check_integrity.py: a Python script that reads manifest.sha256 and verifies each file
  - data/ subdirectory with ~40 files of various types (.txt, .bin, .csv, .json)

- manifest.sha256 was generated correctly at archive time. All hashes in it are accurate for the original file contents.

- The 3 "failing" files are:
  - data/report_2024q1.csv
  - data/config.json  
  - data/notes.txt

- The bug in check_integrity.py is subtle: it opens files in text mode (`open(path, 'r')`) instead of binary mode (`open(path, 'rb')`), then encodes to UTF-8 before hashing. This works for most files but fails for:
  - Files with Windows-style line endings (CRLF): Python's text mode normalizes \r\n to \n on read, changing the content
  - The three "failing" files all contain CRLF line endings (they came from a Windows system originally)
  
- Running `sha256sum data/report_2024q1.csv` from the shell hashes the actual bytes (including \r\n), matching the manifest. The Python script hashes the normalized content (with just \n), producing a different hash.

- Additional misdirection: check_integrity.py has a comment `# handles encoding properly` near the problematic open() call.

- The files are NOT corrupted. The manifest is correct. Only the checker is wrong.

- Python 3.11+, sha256sum, xxd, file, od available. /home/user/audit is fully writable.

Expected final state:
- `python3 /home/user/audit/check_integrity.py` exits 0
- Script prints verification status showing all files pass (exact format may vary but must indicate success)
- All 40+ files in data/ still verify against manifest.sha256 when checked with `cd /home/user/audit && sha256sum -c manifest.sha256` (exits 0, all OK)

Invariants:
- manifest.sha256 must be byte-identical to its original state (the manifest was correct; don't "fix" by regenerating it)
- All files under data/ must be byte-identical to their original state (don't "fix" by converting CRLF to LF)
- check_integrity.py must still exist and be the script that runs for verification

Anti-shortcut guards:
- `sha256sum -c manifest.sha256` must still exit 0 after the fix — this catches any attempt to modify the data files or regenerate the manifest
- `grep -c $'\r'` on the three previously-failing files must return non-zero — they must still contain CRLF (catches normalizing the files instead of fixing the script)
- check_integrity.py must actually read and hash file contents, not just shell out to sha256sum or hardcode results — verified by: temporarily truncating one data file, running the script, and confirming it reports failure
name: task_000000_6bb4e615
